title.zh: 卷二十八上 桓譚馮衍列傳
title.en: Volume 28a: Biographies of Huan Tan; Feng Yan
title.raw: 卷二十八上 桓譚馮衍列傳 Volume 28a: Biographies of Huan Tan; Feng Yan

sentences.zh: 桓譚字君山，沛國相人也。

translations.literal: Huan Tan, courtesy name Junshan, was a man of Xiang in the Principality of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan, styled Junshan, came from Xiang in the princedom of Pei.

sentences.zh: 父成帝時為太樂令。

translations.literal: His father in the time of Chengdi was Director of Grand Music.
translations.idiomatic: His father had been director of grand music under Emperor Cheng.

sentences.zh: 譚以父任為郎，因好音律，善鼓琴。

translations.literal: Tan by his father's privilege became a gentleman, and because he was fond of pitch and mode was skilled at strumming the zither.
translations.idiomatic: Through his father's rank he entered court as a gentleman cadet; he loved music theory and played the zither well.

sentences.zh: 博學多通，遍習《五經》，皆詁訓大義，不為章句。

translations.literal: Broadly learned and broadly conversant, he thoroughly studied the Five Classics, all glossing and explaining great meanings, not doing chapter-and-verse commentary.
translations.idiomatic: He read widely and mastered the Five Classics for their great themes, not for pedantic glosses on single lines.

sentences.zh: 能文章，尤好古學，數從劉歆、楊雄辯析疑異。

translations.literal: He could compose literary pieces, especially loved ancient learning, and repeatedly followed Liu Xin and Yang Xiong in debating and analyzing doubtful points.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote well and doted on antiquarian scholarship, often debating knotty questions with Liu Xin and Yang Xiong.

sentences.zh: 性嗜倡樂，簡易不修威儀，而憙非毀俗儒，由是多見排抵。

translations.literal: By nature he was addicted to entertainers' music, was simple and easy and did not cultivate awe-inspiring deportment, yet delighted in condemning vulgar Confucians, whereby he was often rejected and attacked.
translations.idiomatic: He loved popular entertainments, dressed down without official gravitas, and delighted in mocking pedantic scholars—so he made many enemies.

sentences.zh: 哀、平間，位不過郎。

translations.literal: Between Ai and Ping, his rank did not exceed gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperors Ai and Ping he never rose above gentleman cadet.

sentences.zh: 傅皇后父孔鄉侯晏深善於譚。

translations.literal: The father of Empress Fu, Marquis of Kongxiang Yan, was deeply on good terms with Tan.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Yan, father of Empress Fu and marquis of Kongxiang, was Huan Tan's close friend.

sentences.zh: 是時，高安侯董賢寵倖，女弟為昭儀，皇后日已疏，晏嘿嘿不得意。

translations.literal: At that time Marquis of Gao'an Dong Xian was favored and beloved; his younger sister was Brilliant Companion; the empress daily was already estranged; Yan silently was discontented.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian of Gao'an was the emperor's favorite; his sister was a brilliant companion, while Empress Fu drifted daily into neglect—Fu Yan brooded in silence.

sentences.zh: 譚進說曰：“昔武帝欲立衛子夫，陰求陳皇后之過，而陳後終廢，子夫竟立。

translations.literal: Tan advanced persuasion, saying, "In the past Emperor Wu wished to establish Wei Zifu; he secretly sought faults of Empress Chen, and in the end Empress Chen was deposed and Zifu was at last established.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan urged him: "When Emperor Wu wanted Lady Wei as empress, he secretly hunted faults in Empress Chen until she fell and Wei Zifu took her place."

sentences.zh: 今董賢至愛而女弟尤幸，殆將有子夫之變，可不憂哉！

translations.literal: Now Dong Xian is supremely loved and his younger sister is especially favored—there is peril of a Zifu-style shift; can one not worry!"
translations.idiomatic: Today Dong Xian and his sister hold the inner palace—you may face the same coup in reverse; can you afford not to worry!"

sentences.zh: ”晏驚動，曰：“然，為之奈何？

translations.literal: " Yan was startled and moved, and said, "So—what is to be done about it?
translations.idiomatic: Fu Yan started and said, "True enough—what should we do?"

sentences.zh: ”譚曰：“刑罰不能加無罪，邪枉不能勝正人。

translations.literal: " Tan said, "Punishment cannot be applied to the guiltless; crookedness cannot overcome the upright man.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan answered, "The law cannot strike the innocent, and intrigue cannot defeat an upright heart."

sentences.zh: 夫士以才智要君，女以媚道求主。

translations.literal: The gentleman by wit and wisdom importunes the ruler; the woman by the way of allurement seeks the master.
translations.idiomatic: Men win rulers by talent; women win them by charm.

sentences.zh: 皇后年少，希更艱難，或驅使醫巫，外求方技，此不可不番。

translations.literal: The empress is young, rarely having undergone hardship; perhaps she will be driven to employ physicians and shamans and seek arts outside—this cannot but be examined carefully.
translations.idiomatic: The empress is young and little tested; under strain she may turn to healers, shamans, and occult arts—watch that closely.

sentences.zh: 又君侯以後父尊重而多通賓客，必藉以重勢，貽致譏議。

translations.literal: Moreover, my lord, as the empress's father you are honored and weighty yet widely receive guests; you are sure to borrow thereby to add to your power and bring on ridicule and criticism.
translations.idiomatic: You stand high as the empress's father yet traffic with a wide circle of clients—that borrowed weight will draw slander.

sentences.zh: 不如謝遣門徒，務執廉愨，此修己正家避禍之道也。

translations.literal: It would be better to dismiss your followers and strive to hold incorrupt sincerity—this is the way to cultivate oneself, rectify the family, and avoid calamity.
translations.idiomatic: Better send your hangers-on away and live modestly—that is how to mend your own house and dodge disaster.

translations.literal: " Yan said, "Good."
translations.idiomatic: Fu Yan said, "Well spoken."

sentences.zh: 遂罷遣常客，入白皇后，如譚所戒。

translations.literal: Thereupon he dismissed and sent away his constant guests, entered and reported to the empress, as Tan had warned.
translations.idiomatic: He dismissed his regular clients, entered the palace, and warned the empress as Huan Tan had advised.

sentences.zh: 後賢果風太醫令真欽，使求傅氏罪過，遂逮後弟侍中喜，詔獄無所得，乃解，故傅氏終全於哀帝之時。

translations.literal: Later Xian indeed hinted to Grand Physician-in-chief Zhen Qin, causing him to seek faults of the Fu clan; thereupon they arrested the empress's younger brother, Palace Attendant Xi, but in the edict-ordered prison nothing was obtained, and then it was released; therefore the Fu clan in the end was preserved whole in the time of Emperor Ai.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian later prompted the court physician Zhen Qin to dig up dirt on the Fu family and had the empress's brother Fu Xi, a palace attendant, thrown into the imperial jail—but nothing was proved, so the case collapsed and the Fu clan survived Emperor Ai's reign.

sentences.zh: 及董賢為大司馬，聞譚名，欲與之交。

translations.literal: When Dong Xian became Grand Marshal, he heard Tan's name and wished to befriend him.
translations.idiomatic: When Dong Xian rose to grand marshal he sought Huan Tan's friendship by reputation.

sentences.zh: 譚先奏書於賢，說以輔國保身之術，賢不能用，遂不與通。

translations.literal: Tan first submitted a letter to Xian, explaining to him the arts of assisting the state and preserving the self; Xian could not use it, and thereupon Tan did not communicate with him.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan wrote first, offering Dong Xian ways to serve the state and save himself; Dong Xian ignored the counsel, so Huan Tan refused all further contact.

sentences.zh: 當王莽居攝篡弑之際，天下之士，莫不竟褒稱德美，作符命以求容媚，譚獨自守，默然無言。

translations.literal: At the time when Wang Mang held the regency and usurped and murdered, the gentlemen of the realm none did not compete to praise and declare his virtue and beauty, composing token prophecies to seek accommodation and flattery; Tan alone kept himself and was silent without words.
translations.idiomatic: While Wang Mang acted as regent and seized the throne, every scholar vied to flatter him with forged prophecies; Huan Tan alone stayed silent and kept his integrity.

sentences.zh: 莽時為掌樂大夫，更始立，召拜太中大夫。

translations.literal: In Mang's time he was grandee for music; when Gengshi was established, he was summoned and appointed Grandee of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang he served as grandee for music; the Gengshi Emperor named him grandee of the palace.

sentences.zh: 臣聞國之廢興，在於政事；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that a state's decay and rise lie in government affairs;
translations.idiomatic: Your servant has heard that a realm's rise or fall turns on how it is governed;

sentences.zh: 政事得失，由乎輔佐。

translations.literal: Gain and loss in government affairs come from the assistants.
translations.idiomatic: and whether policy succeeds or fails depends on those who counsel the throne.

sentences.zh: 輔佐賢明，則俊士充朝，而理合世務；

translations.literal: When the assistants are worthy and clear, then outstanding men fill the court, and principle matches the tasks of the age;
translations.idiomatic: When ministers are wise, talent crowds the hall and policy fits the times;

sentences.zh: 輔佐不明，則論失時宜，而舉多過事。

translations.literal: when the assistants are not clear, then discourse misses the timely fit, and recommendations mostly pass beyond the facts.
translations.idiomatic: when they are not, debate misses the moment and every recommendation goes awry.

sentences.zh: 夫有國之君，俱欲興化建善，然而政道未理者，其所謂賢者異也。

translations.literal: The rulers who possess a state all wish to raise transformation and establish good, yet when the way of government is not ordered, it is because what they call worthy differs.
translations.idiomatic: Every sovereign wants good order, yet government stays tangled when his idea of "worthy" men is wrong.

sentences.zh: 昔楚莊王問孫叔敖曰：“寡人未得所以為國是也。

translations.literal: In the past King Zhuang of Chu asked Sunshu Ao, saying, "I have not obtained that by which to decide what is right for the state.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhuang of Chu once asked Sunshu Ao, "I cannot see what truly serves the state."

sentences.zh: ”叔敖曰：“國之有是，眾所惡也，恐王不能定也。

translations.literal: " Shu Ao said, "When a state has a this, it is what the multitude hates; I fear the king cannot settle it.
translations.idiomatic: Sunshu Ao replied, "The 'national consensus' everyone talks about is what the crowd hates—I fear Your Majesty cannot fix it alone."

sentences.zh: ”王曰：“不定獨在君，亦在臣乎？

translations.literal: " The king said, "If it is not settled, is it only in the lord, or also in the ministers?
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "If it stays unsettled, is the fault only mine, or also my ministers'?"

sentences.zh: ”對曰：“居驕士，曰士非我無從富貴；

translations.literal: " He answered, "When the ruler is arrogant toward gentlemen, they say, 'Without me the gentleman has no way to wealth and honor;
translations.idiomatic: He answered, "When a lord sneers at his officers, they say, 'Without us you would never be rich or honored;

sentences.zh: 士驕君，曰君非士無從安存。

translations.literal: when gentlemen are arrogant toward the ruler, they say, 'Without the gentleman the ruler has no way to peace and survival.'
translations.idiomatic: when officers sneer at the throne, they say, 'Without us you could not keep your seat.'"

sentences.zh: 人君或至失國而不悟，士或至饑寒而不進。

translations.literal: Rulers of men sometimes reach the point of losing the state yet do not awaken; gentlemen sometimes reach cold and hunger yet do not advance.
translations.idiomatic: Rulers may lose their realms and still not wake; ministers may starve in the cold and still not step forward.

sentences.zh: 君臣不合，則國是無從定矣。

translations.literal: When ruler and minister do not harmonize, then there is no way for the state's this to be settled.
translations.idiomatic: Until ruler and minister trust each other, no "national consensus" can be settled.

sentences.zh: ”莊王曰：“善。

translations.literal: " King Zhuang said, "Good.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhuang said, "Well said."

sentences.zh: 願相國與諸大夫共定國是也。

translations.literal: I wish the prime minister together with the various grandees jointly to settle what is right for the state.
translations.idiomatic: I ask the chancellor and the high ministers to settle the matter together."

sentences.zh: ”蓋善政者，視俗而施教，察失而立防，威德更興，文武迭用，然後政調于時，而躁人可定。

translations.literal: " Now those who govern well look at custom and then apply teaching, examine loss and then establish safeguards; might and virtue arise in alternation, civil and military are used in rotation—then government is tuned to the times and restless men can be settled.
translations.idiomatic: Good government reads the temper of the people before teaching them, spots weakness before building dikes, and alternates kindness with firmness, culture with force—then the age is tuned and the restless are quieted.

sentences.zh: 昔董仲舒言“理國譬若琴瑟，其不調者則解而更張”。

translations.literal: In the past Dong Zhongshu said, 'Ordering a state is like the zither and se—where they are not in tune, untie and stretch anew.'
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu said, "Governing is like tuning zither and se: when the notes clash, you loosen the strings and reset them."

sentences.zh: 夫更張難行，而拂眾者亡，是故賈誼以才逐，而朝錯以智死。

translations.literal: Now stretching anew is hard to carry out, and those who brush the multitude perish; therefore Jia Yi by talent was driven off, and Chao Cuo by wisdom died.
translations.idiomatic: But retuning is dangerous work: cross the crowd and you perish—Jia Yi was banished for his gifts, Chao Cuo died for his cleverness.

sentences.zh: 世雖有殊能而終莫敢談者，懼於前事也。

translations.literal: Though the age has unusual ability, in the end none dares speak—fear of what went before.
translations.idiomatic: So men of unusual gifts hold their tongues, terrified by those precedents.

sentences.zh: 且設法禁者，非能盡塞天下之奸，皆合眾人之所欲也，大抵取便國利事多者，則可矣。

translations.literal: Moreover, in setting up laws and prohibitions, one cannot exhaustively block all villainy in the realm or make them all match what the multitude desires; broadly, if what mostly profits the state and its affairs is taken, then it suffices.
translations.idiomatic: Laws cannot plug every crime or please every wish; if they usually help the state, that is enough.

sentences.zh: 夫張官置吏，以理萬人，縣賞設罰，以別善惡，惡人誅傷，則善人蒙福矣。

translations.literal: Now spreading offices and setting clerks is to order the myriad people; hanging rewards and setting punishments is to distinguish good and bad; when the evil are executed and wounded, then the good receive blessing.
translations.idiomatic: Offices exist to govern the people; rewards and punishments mark right from wrong; strike down the wicked and the good prosper.

sentences.zh: 今人相殺傷，雖已伏法，而私結怨仇，子孫相報，後忿深前，至於滅戶殄業，而俗稱豪健，故雖有怯弱，猶勉而行之，此為聽人自理而無複法禁者也。

translations.literal: Now people kill and wound one another; although they have already submitted to the law, yet privately they knot resentment and hatred, and sons and grandsons avenge one another; later hate deepens what went before, to the point of extinguishing households and destroying livelihoods, while custom praises them as bold and stalwart; therefore even though there are timid and weak ones, they still force themselves to do it—this is to listen to people ordering themselves for themselves and again to have no legal prohibition.
translations.idiomatic: Men kill each other, pay the legal price, yet private feuds pass down the generations until whole clans are wiped out—while local opinion hails the avengers as heroes, so even cowards feel shamed into bloodshed. That means letting private vengeance replace the law.

sentences.zh: 今宜申明舊令，若已伏官誅而私相傷殺者，雖一身逃亡，皆徙家屬于邊，其相傷者，加常二等，不得雇山贖罪。

translations.literal: Now it is fitting clearly to proclaim the old order: if after one has already submitted to official execution they privately wound and kill one another, although a single person flees, all remove the family dependents to the frontier; for those who wound one another, add two degrees beyond the constant rule, and do not permit hiring mountain (labour) to redeem the crime.
translations.idiomatic: Reissue the old statute: anyone who murders for private revenge after the state has already punished the original killer, even a lone fugitive, shall see his whole family banished to the frontier; private assailants get two extra degrees of sentence, with no commutation by convict labor in the hill works.

sentences.zh: 如此，則仇怨自解，盜賊息矣。

translations.literal: In this way, then, hatred and resentment will of themselves dissolve, and banditry will cease.
translations.idiomatic: Do that, and feuds will cool and brigandage die down.

sentences.zh: 夫理國之道，舉本業而抑末利，是以先帝禁人二業，錮商賈不得宦為吏，此所以抑並兼長廉恥也。

translations.literal: Now the way to order a state is to lift the root occupation and suppress secondary profit; therefore the former emperors forbade people two occupations, and barred merchants from serving as clerks or officials—this is the means by which to check annexation and lengthen incorrupt shame.
translations.idiomatic: Good government promotes farming and checks secondary trades: past emperors barred dual occupations and kept merchants from office to stop land-grabbing and teach shame.

sentences.zh: 今富商大賈，多放錢貨，中家子弟，為之保役，趨走與臣僕等勤，收稅與封君比入，是以眾人慕效，不耕而食，至乃多通侈靡，以淫耳目。

translations.literal: Now great merchants mostly lend money at interest; sons of middling families act as their guarantors and labor, running about with diligence equal to servants and slaves; they collect taxes and compare income with enfeoffed lords—therefore the multitude admires and imitates, not plowing yet eating, until they widely traffic in extravagance and waste to glut eyes and ears.
translations.idiomatic: Great merchants lend at interest; sons of modest families become their bondsmen and runners, earning sums that rival titled lords—so the crowd apes them, eats without plowing, and sinks into vulgar display.

sentences.zh: 今可令諸商賈自相糾告，若非身力所得，皆以臧界告者。

translations.literal: Now it is possible to order the various merchants mutually to inform and accuse; if it was not obtained by one's bodily labor, all take it as corruption goods to the accuser.
translations.idiomatic: Order merchants to inform on one another: any unearned gain is forfeit to the informer.

sentences.zh: 如此，則專役一已，不敢以貨與人，事寡力弱，必歸功田畝。

translations.literal: In this way, then they will toil for themselves alone and not dare give goods to others; affairs will be few and strength weak, and they will necessarily return merit to fields and acres.
translations.idiomatic: Then each will sweat for himself alone and lend nothing abroad; weak in other trades, they will drift back to the plow.

sentences.zh: 田畝修，則穀入多而地力盡矣。

translations.literal: When fields and acres are put in order, then grain income is much and land's power is exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Tend the fields well and the granaries fill while the soil gives its utmost.

sentences.zh: 又見法令決事，輕重不齊，或一事殊法，同罪異論，奸吏得因緣為市，所欲活則出生議，所欲陷則與死比，是為刑開二門也。

translations.literal: Moreover, your servant sees that when laws and orders decide affairs, light and heavy are not uniform, or one affair has different laws, same crime different discussion, and wicked clerks can thereby make a market: whom they wish to live they produce a living proposal; whom they wish to trap they compare to death—this is to open two gates for punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant also sees judgments skewed—one crime, many verdicts—so corrupt clerks sell justice: spare a friend, hang a foe. That opens two doors to the penal code.

sentences.zh: 今可令通義理明習法律者，校定科比，一其法度，班下郡國，蠲除故條。

translations.literal: Now it is possible to order those who comprehend principles and clearly practice the law to collate statutes and comparisons, unify their measure of law, promulgate down to commanderies and principalities, and abolish former articles.
translations.idiomatic: Commission learned jurists to collate statutes, unify the code, publish it to every commandery, and repeal obsolete clauses.

sentences.zh: 如此，天下知方，而獄無怨濫矣。

translations.literal: In this way, the realm will know the square, and prisons will have no resentful excess.
translations.idiomatic: Then the empire will know the rules, and jails will not brim with wrongful sentences.

sentences.zh: 書奏，不省。

translations.literal: The memorial was submitted and not examined.
translations.idiomatic: He submitted the memorial; the emperor never read it.

sentences.zh: 是時，帝方信讖，多以決定嫌疑。

translations.literal: At that time the emperor was trusting prophecy texts and mostly used them to decide doubtful points.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was trusting apocryphal prophecies to settle every doubt.

sentences.zh: 又酬賞少薄，天下不時安定。

translations.literal: Moreover rewards for merit were scant and thin; the realm was not timely settled.
translations.idiomatic: Rewards for merit were meager, and the land knew no lasting peace.

sentences.zh: 譚複上疏曰：臣前獻瞽言，未蒙詔報，不勝憤懣，冒死得陳。

translations.literal: Tan again submitted a memorial, saying: Your servant earlier presented blind words and did not receive an edict in reply; unable to overcome indignation, he braves death to state them.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan wrote again: "I spoke once without answer; unable to contain my grief, I risk death to repeat it."

sentences.zh: 愚夫策謀，有益於政道者，以合人心而得事理也。

translations.literal: The foolish man's plans and schemes, if they benefit the way of government, are because they match human hearts and obtain the pattern of affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Even a commoner's plan helps government when it fits human nature and the facts.

sentences.zh: 凡人情忽於見事而貴于異聞，觀先王之所記述，咸以仁義正道為本，非有奇怪虛誕之事。

translations.literal: As a rule human feeling neglects what is before the eyes and esteems strange hearsay; observing what former kings recorded and wrote, all took benevolence, righteousness, and the correct way as root; they had no strange, empty, and false matters.
translations.idiomatic: Men dismiss what they see daily and chase marvels; yet the sages wrote only of humanity, duty, and the straight path—never of empty wonders.

sentences.zh: 蓋天道性命，聖人所難言也。

translations.literal: Now the way of Heaven, nature, and destiny is what sages find hard to speak of.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's way, fate, and human nature are what even sages discuss rarely.

sentences.zh: 自子貢以下，不得而聞，況後世淺儒，能通之乎！

translations.literal: From Zigong downward, they could not obtain hearing of it—how much less can shallow Confucians of later ages penetrate it!
translations.idiomatic: Below Zigong's generation disciples could not grasp them—how shall shallow scholars of our day!

sentences.zh: 今諸巧慧小才伎數之人，增益圖書，矯稱讖記，以欺惑貪邪，詿誤人主，焉可不抑遠之哉！

translations.literal: Now the various clever, petty-talented, artful-number people add to and enlarge charts and books, falsely style them prophecy records, thereby to deceive and delude the greedy and evil, and lead the ruler astray—how can they not be suppressed and kept at distance!
translations.idiomatic: Today clever charlatans pad apocryphal books, call them prophecy, and beguile greedy minds and mislead the throne—they must be checked and banished!

sentences.zh: 臣譚伏聞陛下窮折方士黃白之術，甚為明矣；

translations.literal: Your servant Tan has heard with bowed head that Your Majesty has thoroughly refuted the alchemists' arts of yellow and white—this is very enlightened;
translations.idiomatic: Your servant hears that Your Majesty has exposed the alchemists' gold-and-silver fraud—most enlightened;

sentences.zh: 而乃欲聽納讖記，又何誤也！

translations.literal: yet you would listen to and accept prophecy records—what error again!
translations.idiomatic: yet you would heed forged prophecies—how is that less a mistake!

sentences.zh: 其事雖有時合，譬猶卜數隻偶之類。

translations.literal: Though their affairs sometimes match, it is like divination by numbers hitting by chance on a few.
translations.idiomatic: When they occasionally prove true, it is mere chance, like a lucky cast of the milfoil.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜垂明聽，發聖意，屏群小之曲說，述《五經》之正義，略雷同之俗語，詳通人之雅謀。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought to let fall clear hearing, issue sagely intention, screen the twisted sayings of the petty crowd, pursue the correct meaning of the Five Classics, omit vulgar sayings of mere echo, and examine thoroughly the elegant plans of thorough-going men.
translations.idiomatic: Open your ears, issue clear intent, cast off petty occultism, return to the plain sense of the Five Classics, drop parroted slogans, and weigh the counsel of true scholars.

sentences.zh: 又臣聞安平則尊道術之士，有難則貴介胄之臣。

translations.literal: Moreover your servant has heard that in peace one honors masters of the Way and arts; in hardship one esteems ministers in armor.
translations.idiomatic: In peace a state honors scholars; in peril it prizes generals in mail.

sentences.zh: 今聖朝興複祖統，為人臣主，而四方盜賊未盡歸伏者，此權謀未得也。

translations.literal: Now the sagely court has raised anew the ancestral system and is lord to human ministers, yet the bandits of the four quarters have not all submitted and submitted—this is that stratagems and plans have not been obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Your restored Han rules the ministers of the realm, yet rebels still hold out—because strategy has failed.

sentences.zh: 臣譚伏觀陛下用兵，諸所降下，既無重賞以相恩誘，或至虜掠奪其財物，是以兵長渠率，各生孤疑，黨輩連結，歲月不解。

translations.literal: Your servant Tan has bowed his head and observed Your Majesty's use of troops: in all those who have surrendered, there is neither heavy reward to entice them with favor, or sometimes to the point of capturing and plundering and seizing their goods and property—therefore the army chiefs and band leaders each produce solitary doubt, cliques and factions link and connect, and years and months do not resolve.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant sees how you treat capitulation: no rich reward wins them over, while soldiers often loot them bare—so chieftains hesitate, bands league together, and the war drags year on year.

sentences.zh: 古人有言曰：“天下皆知取之為取，而莫知與之為取。

translations.literal: The ancients had a saying: "All under heaven know that taking is taking, yet none know that giving is taking.
translations.idiomatic: The ancients said, "The world calls seizing 'gain,' but forgets that giving is also gain."

sentences.zh: ”陛下誠能輕爵重賞，與士共之，則何招而不至，何說而不釋，何向而不開，何征而不克！

translations.literal: " If Your Majesty can truly make light noble rank and weighty reward, and share with gentlemen, then what will you summon and they not arrive, what persuade and they not release, what direction and they not open, what campaign and they not conquer!
translations.idiomatic: Lighten titles, heap up rewards, and share spoils with your men—then whom could you not summon, what knot not untie, what door not open, what foe not break!

sentences.zh: 如此，則能以狹為廣，以遲為速，亡者複存，失者複得矣。

translations.literal: In this way, then you can take narrow for broad, take slow for fast; the perished again exist, the lost again are obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Then you turn scant means into plenty, delay into speed, restore what seemed lost, recover what seemed gone.

sentences.zh: 帝省奏，愈不悅。

translations.literal: The emperor examined the memorial and was increasingly displeased.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read it and liked him even less.

sentences.zh: 其後，有詔會議靈台所處，帝謂譚曰：“吾欲以讖決之，何如？

translations.literal: Afterward there was an edict to convene discussion on where to place the Ling Terrace; the emperor said to Tan, "I wish to use prophecy to decide it—how would that be?
translations.idiomatic: Later, debating where to build the Ling Terrace, the emperor asked Huan Tan, "Shall I let the prophecies decide?"

sentences.zh: ”譚默然良久，曰：“臣不讀讖。

translations.literal: " Tan was silent a long time and said, "Your servant does not read prophecy texts.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan was silent, then said, "I do not study apocrypha."

sentences.zh: ”帝問其故，譚複極言讖之非經。

translations.literal: " The emperor asked the reason; Tan again spoke to the utmost that prophecy is not canonical.
translations.idiomatic: Asked why, he said flatly that such books are not classical.

sentences.zh: 帝大怒曰：“桓譚非聖無法，將下斬之！

translations.literal: The emperor was greatly angry and said, "Huan Tan denies the sages and has no law—take him down and behead him!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor roared, "Huan Tan rejects the sages and spurns the law—off with his head!"

sentences.zh: ”譚叩頭流血，良久乃得解。

translations.literal: " Tan knocked his head until it bled; after a long time he then obtained release.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan knocked his brow bloody on the stones until the rage cooled.

sentences.zh: 出為六安郡丞，意忽忽不樂，道病卒，時年七十餘。

translations.literal: He went out as assistant to the grand administrator of Lu'an commandery; his mind was absent and unhappy; on the road he died of illness, aged over seventy.
translations.idiomatic: They sent him out as assistant governor of Lu'an; he went in a daze of grief and died on the road, past seventy.

sentences.zh: 初，譚著書言當世行事二十九篇，號曰《新論》，上書獻之，世祖善焉。

translations.literal: At first Tan had composed a book on the conduct of the present age in twenty-nine sections, titled New Discourses, and submitted it by memorial; Shizu praised it.
translations.idiomatic: He had written twenty-nine essays on his own times, the New Discussions, which Emperor Guangwu praised.

sentences.zh: 《琴道》一篇未成，肅宗使班固續成之。

translations.literal: One piece, The Way of the Zither, was not completed; Emperor Suzong ordered Ban Gu to continue and complete it.
translations.idiomatic: The essay The Way of the Zither was unfinished; Emperor Zhang told Ban Gu to complete it.

sentences.zh: 所著賦、誄、書、奏，凡二十六篇。

translations.literal: What he composed in rhapsodies, dirges, letters, and memorials altogether numbered twenty-six pieces.
translations.idiomatic: His rhapsodies, dirges, letters, and memorials number twenty-six pieces in all.

sentences.zh: 元和中，肅宗行東巡狩，至沛，使使者祠譚塚，鄉里以為榮。

translations.literal: In the Yuanhe period, when Emperor Suzong traveled east on inspection hunt to Pei, he sent envoys to sacrifice at Tan's tomb; the village and neighborhood took it as honor.
translations.idiomatic: During Yuanhe, Emperor Zhang toured east to Pei and sent envoys to sacrifice at Huan Tan's tomb—a glory for his hometown.

sentences.zh: 馮衍字敬通，京兆杜陵人也。

translations.literal: Feng Yan, courtesy name Jingtong, was a man of Duling in the metropolitan region of Jingzhao.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan, styled Jingtong, came from Duling in the Jingzhao capital district.

sentences.zh: 祖野王，元帝時為大鴻臚。

translations.literal: His grandfather Yewang, in the time of Yuandi, was Grand Herald.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Feng Yewang had been grand herald under Emperor Yuan.

sentences.zh: 衍幼有奇才，年九歲，能誦《詩》，至二十而博通群書。

translations.literal: Yan in youth had unusual talent; at nine sui he could recite the Odes; reaching twenty he was broadly conversant with all books.
translations.idiomatic: A prodigy, he knew the Odes by heart at nine and had read everything worth reading by twenty.

sentences.zh: 王莽時，諸公多薦舉之者，衍辭不肯仕。

translations.literal: In the time of Wang Mang, the various dukes mostly recommended him; Yan declined and was unwilling to serve.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang's high ministers recommended him repeatedly; he refused office.

sentences.zh: 時，天下兵起，莽遣更始將軍廉丹討伐山東。

translations.literal: At that time military uprising arose in the realm; Mang sent General Gengshi Lian Dan to campaign east of the mountains.
translations.idiomatic: When rebellion flared, Wang Mang sent General Gengshi Lian Dan east of the Taihang range.

sentences.zh: 丹辟衍為掾，與俱至定陶。

translations.literal: Dan summoned Yan as clerk and with him reached Dingtao.
translations.idiomatic: Lian Dan made Feng Yan his clerk and took him to Dingtao.

sentences.zh: 莽追詔丹曰：“倉廩盡矣，府庫空矣，可以怒矣，可以戰矣。

translations.literal: Mang pursued with an edict to Dan, saying, "The granaries are exhausted, the treasuries are empty; one may be angry, one may fight.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang's follow-up edict read, "Granaries are bare and treasuries empty—anger is due, battle is due.

sentences.zh: 將軍受國重任，不捐身於中野，無以報恩塞責。

translations.literal: The general has received the state's heavy burden; if you do not cast away your body in the wilds of the field, there is no means to repay favor and stop responsibility.
translations.idiomatic: You bear the state's heavy charge: unless you die in the field you cannot repay it or answer for it."

sentences.zh: ”丹惶恐，夜召衍，以書示之。

translations.literal: " Dan was fearful and alarmed; at night he summoned Yan and showed him the letter.
translations.idiomatic: Lian Dan was terrified; that night he called Feng Yan and showed him the letter.

sentences.zh: 衍因說丹曰：“衍聞順而成者，道之所大也；

translations.literal: Yan thereupon persuaded Dan, saying, "Your servant Yan has heard that what follows the current and completes is what the Way greatly esteems;
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan answered, "I have heard that success won with the tide is what the Way most honors;

sentences.zh: 逆而功者，權之所貴也。

translations.literal: what opposes the current yet achieves merit is what expediency esteems.
translations.idiomatic: success won against the tide is what circumstance most prizes.

sentences.zh: 是故期於有成，不問所由；

translations.literal: Therefore one aims at having achievement, not asking by what path;
translations.idiomatic: So aim at the outcome, not the path;

sentences.zh: 論於大體，不守小節。

translations.literal: debating the great frame, not guarding petty points.
translations.idiomatic: weigh the great pattern, not petty scruples.

sentences.zh: 昔逢醜父伏軾而使其君取飲，稱于諸侯；

translations.literal: In the past Feng Choufu leaned on the carriage bar and made his ruler go for a drink, and was praised among the feudal lords;
translations.idiomatic: Feng Choufu once hid his lord under the chariot rail and was praised in the odes;

sentences.zh: 鄭祭仲立突而出忽，終得復位，美於《春秋》。

translations.literal: Zhai Zhong of Zheng raised Prince Tu and expelled Prince Hu, and in the end the rightful heir was restored—an act the Spring and Autumn praises.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Zhong of Zheng set Prince Tu on the throne and drove Prince Hu into exile, then restored right order—Spring and Autumn judges him kindly."

sentences.zh: 蓋以死易生，以存易亡，君子之道也。

translations.literal: For thus to exchange death for life, exchange perishing for survival, is the way of the gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: To trade certain death for a chance at life is the gentleman's path.

sentences.zh: 詭于眾意，甯國存身，賢智之慮也。

translations.literal: To deceive the multitude's intent yet settle the state and preserve the self is the consideration of the worthy and wise.
translations.idiomatic: Bend to popular opinion yet save the realm and your skin—that is what a wise man weighs.

sentences.zh: 故《易》曰‘窮則變，變則通，通則久，是以自天祐之，吉，無不利’。

translations.literal: Therefore the Changes says, 'When exhausted, then change; when changed, then penetrate; when penetrated, then long enduring—therefore from Heaven there is blessing, auspicious, without nothing not favorable.'
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Changes says: 'Blockage forces change, change opens the way, an open way endures'—Heaven itself blesses that course.

sentences.zh: 若夫知其不可而必行之，破軍殘眾，無補於主，身死之日，負義于時，智者不為，勇者不行。

translations.literal: As for knowing it cannot be done yet necessarily doing it, breaking the army and maiming the host, without benefit to the lord, on the day of one's death bearing unrighteousness toward the age—the wise do not do it, the brave do not carry it out.
translations.idiomatic: To charge into a hopeless fight, waste your army, die disgraced, and help your lord not at all—no wise or brave man chooses that.

sentences.zh: 且衍聞之，得時無怠。

translations.literal: Moreover your servant Yan has heard: obtaining the moment, do not slack.
translations.idiomatic: And I say: when Heaven offers an opening, seize it without delay.

sentences.zh: 張良以五世相韓，椎秦始皇博浪之中，勇冠乎賁、育，名高乎太山。

translations.literal: Zhang Liang, with five generations serving as minister to Han, struck at the First Emperor of Qin amid the waves at Bolang; his courage crowned Ben and Yu; his name was higher than Mount Tai.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang, whose forebears had served the state of Han for five generations as ministers, struck at the First Emperor in the surf at Bolang—courage beyond warriors Ben and Yu, fame higher than Taishan.

sentences.zh: 將軍之先，為漢信臣。

translations.literal: The general's ancestors were trusted ministers of Han.
translations.idiomatic: Your own ancestors were loyal servants of the Han house.

sentences.zh: 新室之興，英俊不附。

translations.literal: At the rise of the New house, outstanding men did not attach.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang founded Xin, no true talent rallied to him.

sentences.zh: 今海內潰亂，人懷漢德，甚于詩人思召公也，愛其甘棠，而況子孫乎？

translations.literal: Now within the seas there is collapse and disorder; people cherish Han virtue more than the poets longed for Duke Shao—they loved his sweet pear tree—how much more their descendants?
translations.idiomatic: The realm is in ruins, yet the people cling to Han as the poets clung to the memory of the Duke of Shao under the sweet pear—how much more his heirs?

sentences.zh: 人所歌舞，天必從之。

translations.literal: What men sing and dance, Heaven is sure to follow.
translations.idiomatic: What the people celebrate, Heaven seconds.

sentences.zh: 方今為將軍計，莫若屯據大郡，鎮撫吏士，砥厲其節，百里之內，牛酒日賜，納雄桀之士，詢忠智之謀，要將來之心，待從橫之變，興社稷之利，除萬人之害，則福祿流於無窮，功烈著於不滅。

translations.literal: For the present, to plan for the general, nothing is better than to encamp and hold a great commandery, pacify and soothe clerks and soldiers, grind sharp their integrity, within a hundred li daily bestow oxen and wine, take in bold and outstanding gentlemen, inquire into loyal and wise plans, seize the hearts of those who will come, await the changes of vertical and horizontal alliances, raise the altars of soil and grain to profit, remove the harm of the myriad people—then blessing and salary will flow without end, merit and blaze be inscribed imperishable.
translations.idiomatic: My counsel: occupy a rich commandery, hearten your troops, feast the brave within a hundred li, gather wise counsel, win wavering lords, watch how the chessboard shifts, then strike for the altars' good and the people's relief—your name will shine forever.

sentences.zh: 何與軍覆于中原，身膏於草野，功敗名喪，恥及先祖哉？

translations.literal: What of having the army overturned in the central plain, your body rendered fat on the wild grass, merit defeated and name lost, shame reaching the former ancestors?
translations.idiomatic: Why choose instead total defeat on the plain, your corpse crow-bait, honor lost and ancestors shamed?

sentences.zh: 聖人轉禍而為福，智士因敗而為功，願明公深計而無與俗同。

translations.literal: Sages turn calamity into blessing; wise men by defeat achieve merit—I hope my lord will think deeply and not be the same as the vulgar.
translations.idiomatic: Sages turn disaster to gain; wise men ride defeat to victory—think hard, general, and do not drift with the crowd.

sentences.zh: ”丹不能從。

translations.literal: " Dan could not follow.
translations.idiomatic: Lian Dan would not listen.

sentences.zh: 進及睢陽，複說丹曰：“蓋聞明者見於無形，智者慮于未萌，況其昭{折曰}者乎？

translations.literal: Advancing to Suiyang, he again persuaded Dan, saying, "Your servant has heard that the clear-sighted see in what has no form, the wise plan before buds sprout—how much more when it is already plain?
translations.idiomatic: Near Suiyang he urged again: "The far-sighted read signs before they form; the wise act before crisis buds—how much more when the danger is plain?"

sentences.zh: 凡患生於所忽，禍發於細微，敗不可悔，時不可失。

translations.literal: As a rule trouble is born from what is neglected; disaster springs from the minute; defeat cannot be regretted, the moment cannot be lost.
translations.idiomatic: Misfortune grows from small neglect; disaster sparks from a spark; defeat allows no second chance, and the hour waits for no man.

sentences.zh: 公孫鞅曰：‘有高人之行，負非於世；

translations.literal: Gongsun Yang said, 'To have conduct surpassing other men is to bear blame from the world;
translations.idiomatic: Shang Yang said, 'Rise above the herd and the world calls you proud;

sentences.zh: 有獨見之慮，見贅於人。

translations.literal: to have plans of solitary insight is to be deemed superfluous by others.'
translations.idiomatic: think for yourself and men call you odd.'"

sentences.zh: ’故信庸庸之論，破金石之策，襲當世之操，失高明之德。

translations.literal: 'Therefore to trust commonplace opinions is to smash a plan hard as metal and stone; to follow the conduct of the age is to lose virtue that is high and bright.
translations.idiomatic: Trust the mob and you shatter a policy sound as bronze; ape fashion and you forfeit a noble name.

sentences.zh: 夫決者智之君也。

translations.literal: Now decision is the lord of wisdom.
translations.idiomatic: Resolution commands wisdom.

sentences.zh: 疑者事之役也。

translations.literal: Doubt is the servant of affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Hesitation enslaves every enterprise.

sentences.zh: 時不重至，公勿再計。

translations.literal: The moment does not come twice; my lord, do not deliberate again.
translations.idiomatic: Fortune's tide will not return—do not debate twice.

sentences.zh: ”丹不聽，遂進及無鹽，與赤眉戰死。

translations.literal: " Dan did not listen; thereupon advancing he reached Wuyan and died in battle with the Red Eyebrows.
translations.idiomatic: Lian Dan marched on, met the Red Eyebrows at Wuyan, and died on the field.

sentences.zh: 衍乃亡命河東。

translations.literal: Yan thereupon fled for his life to Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan fled to Hedong as a fugitive.

sentences.zh: 更始二年，遣尚書僕射鮑永行大將軍事，安集北方。

translations.literal: In the second year of Gengshi, he sent Vice Director of the Secretariat Bao Yong to act as grand marshal and pacify the north.
translations.idiomatic: In Gengshi 2 the court sent Bao Yong, vice director of the secretariat, north as acting grand marshal to settle the region.

sentences.zh: 衍因以計說永曰：

translations.literal: Yan thereupon by stratagem persuaded Yong, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan laid a strategy before Bao Yong:

sentences.zh: 衍聞明君不惡切愨之言，以測幽冥之論；

translations.literal: Your servant Yan has heard that the enlightened ruler does not dislike cutting and earnest words, by which to fathom dark and hidden arguments;
translations.idiomatic: An enlightened prince welcomes blunt speech to sound deep truth;

sentences.zh: 忠臣不顧爭引之患，以達萬機之變。

translations.literal: the loyal minister does not look to the harm of contentious remonstrance, in order to penetrate the myriad engines' changes.
translations.idiomatic: a loyal minister risks offense to catch every shift in the machinery of state.

sentences.zh: 是故君臣兩興，功名兼立，銘勒金石，令問不忘。

translations.literal: Therefore ruler and minister both rise, merit and name are jointly established, inscriptions are cut in metal and stone, and fine repute is not forgotten.
translations.idiomatic: Then ruler and minister rise together, win lasting fame, and merit is graven in bronze, not forgotten.

sentences.zh: 今衍幸逢寬明之日，將值危言之時，豈敢拱默避罪，而不竭其誠哉！

translations.literal: Now your servant Yan has luckily encountered a lenient and enlightened day and will meet a time of perilous words—how dare he fold his arms in silence and avoid blame, and not exhaust his sincerity!
translations.idiomatic: I meet a tolerant court and a time that calls for dangerous truths—shall I hold my tongue to save my skin?

sentences.zh: 伏念天上離王莽之害久矣。

translations.literal: Bowed in thought: all under heaven has long been parted from Wang Mang's harm.
translations.idiomatic: All under heaven has suffered Wang Mang's scourge long enough.

sentences.zh: 始自東郡之師，繼以西海之役，巴、蜀沒于南夷，緣邊破于北狄，遠征萬里，暴兵累年，禍拏未解，兵連不息，刑法彌深，賦斂愈重。

translations.literal: Beginning with the army from Dong commandery, continuing to the campaign on the western sea, Ba and Shu sank in the southern barbarians, the border was broken by the northern Di, distant expeditions ten thousand li, exposed troops piled year on year, the grip of calamity not loosened, weapons linked without cease, punishments grew ever deeper, taxes and levies grew heavier.
translations.idiomatic: From the eastern campaigns to the western wars, Shu swallowed by southern tribes, the frontier broken by the Xiongnu, armies abroad for years, taxes crushing, the law a tightening noose—

sentences.zh: 眾強之黨，橫擊於外，百僚之臣，貪殘於內，元元無聊，饑寒並臻，父子流亡，夫婦離散，廬落丘墟，田疇蕪穢，疾疫大興，災異蜂起。

translations.literal: Factions of the powerful struck outward; the hundred officials' ministers were greedy and cruel within; the common folk had no relief, hunger and cold arrived together, fathers and sons wandered in exile, husbands and wives were scattered, hamlets became mounds and ruins, fields were overgrown and foul, great pestilence arose, prodigies swarmed like bees.
translations.idiomatic: —strongmen rampaged abroad, officials devoured within, the people starved and froze, families broke, villages turned to ash, plague and omen followed one another.

sentences.zh: 於是江湖之上，海岱之濱，風騰波湧，更相駘藉，四垂之人，肝腦塗地，死亡之數，不啻太半，殃咎之毒，痛入骨髓，匹夫僮婦，鹹懷怨怒。

translations.literal: Thereupon on the Yangtze and the lakes, along the coast of sea and Mount Dai, wind rose and waves surged, trampling one another in succession; the people of the four margins smeared liver and brains on the earth; the count of the dead was not less than more than half; the poison of disaster and fault bit into marrow and bone; common men and slave wives all harbored resentment and anger.
translations.idiomatic: Along the great rivers and eastern coast mobs surged and trampled one another; beyond the heartland half the people were dead, hatred gnawed to the bone, every peasant and bondmaid nursed rage.

sentences.zh: 皇帝以聖德靈威，龍興鳳舉，率宛、葉之眾，將散亂之兵，喢血昆陽，長驅武關，破百萬之陳，摧九虎之軍，雷震四海，席捲天下，攘除禍亂，誅滅無道，一期之間，海內大定。

translations.literal: The emperor by sagely virtue and numinous might, dragon rising and phoenix lifting, leading the hosts of Wan and Ye, commanding scattered troops, gnawed blood at Kunyang, drove long at Wuguan, broke formations of a million, crushed the army of the Nine Tigers, thunder shook the four seas, rolled up the realm like a mat, swept away disaster and disorder, executed and destroyed the lawless—in the space of one cycle within the seas there was great peace.
translations.idiomatic: Then our emperor rose like dragon and phoenix, led the men of Wan and Ye, shattered a million foemen at Kunyang, stormed Wuguan, broke the Nine Tigers—thunder across the empire, the realm rolled up in one sweep, the wicked cut down, and in a single turning the land knew peace.

sentences.zh: 繼高祖之休烈，修文武之絕業，社稷複存，炎精更輝，德冠往初，功無與二。

translations.literal: He continued Gaozu's splendid blaze, repaired the cut thread of Wen and Wu's work; the altars of soil and grain again stood, the fiery essence of Han blazed anew; virtue crowned all beginnings, merit matched by none.
translations.idiomatic: He took up Gaozu's torch, restored the broken work of Wen and Wu, relit Han's red mandate, outshone every predecessor, matched by no successor.

sentences.zh: 天下自以去亡新，就聖漢，當蒙其福而賴其願。

translations.literal: All under heaven by themselves thought they had cast off perished Xin and drawn near to sagely Han; they ought to receive its blessing and rely on its wish.
translations.idiomatic: The people thought they had shed Xin and come home to Han, ready for mercy and reward.

sentences.zh: 樹恩布德，易以周洽，其猶順驚風而飛鴻毛也。

translations.literal: To plant favor and spread virtue is easy to reach everywhere; it is still like following a startled wind and flying goose feathers.
translations.idiomatic: Kindness shown now would spread as fast as downwind feathers.

sentences.zh: 然而諸將虜掠，逆倫絕理，殺人父子，妻人婦女，燔其室屋，略其財產，饑者毛食，寒者裸跣，冤結失望，無所歸命。

translations.literal: Yet the various generals plundered, reversed norm and cut principle, killed people's fathers and sons, took others' wives and women, burned their houses, seized their goods and property; the hungry gnawed scraps, the cold went naked and barefoot; wrongs knotted and hope failed, there was nowhere to turn for life.
translations.idiomatic: Yet your generals looted, murdered fathers and sons, seized wives, torched homes, stripped the starving and shamed the cold until the people had nowhere to appeal.

sentences.zh: 今大將軍以明淑之德，秉大使之權，統三軍之政，存撫并州之人，惠愛之誠，加乎百姓，高世之聲，聞乎群士，故其延頸企踵而望者，非特一人也。

translations.literal: Now the grand marshal by enlightened and pure virtue holds the great envoy's authority, commands the three armies' government, cherishes and soothes the people of Bing province; the sincerity of kindness and love extends to the hundred surnames; the sound of transcending the age is heard among the host of gentlemen—therefore those who crane necks and stand on tiptoe in hope are not merely one man.
translations.idiomatic: You, great marshal, are clear in virtue, hold imperial mandate, command three armies, cherish Bingzhou—the people strain to hear one kind word from you; you are not speaking to a single listener.

sentences.zh: 且大將軍之事，豈得珪璧其行，束修其心而已哉？

translations.literal: Moreover can the grand marshal's task be only to polish his conduct like a jade tablet and bind his mind with a bundle of dried meat?
translations.idiomatic: A grand marshal's charge is not merely to polish his manners like a ritual jade.

sentences.zh: 將定國家之大業，成天地之元功也。

translations.literal: He will settle the state's great enterprise and complete the primordial merit of Heaven and earth.
translations.idiomatic: He must settle the realm and finish the work Heaven and earth began.

sentences.zh: 昔周宣中興之主，齊桓霸強之君耳，猶有申伯、召虎、夷吾、吉甫攘其蝥賊，安其疆宇。

translations.literal: In the past King Xuan of Zhou was a ruler who restored the middle; Duke Huan of Qi was a lord who hegemonized by strength—yet still they had Shen Bo, Zhao Hu, Yiguan, and Jifu to drive off their pests and settle their borders.
translations.idiomatic: King Xuan and Duke Huan were only restorer and hegemon, yet they needed Shen Bo, Zhao Hu, Guan Zhong, and Yin Jifu to scourge pests and secure the frontier.

sentences.zh: 況乎萬里之漢，明帝復興，而大將軍為之梁棟，此誠不可以忽也。

translations.literal: How much more the Han of ten thousand li, the enlightened emperor again rising, while the grand marshal is its beam and pillar—this truly cannot be neglected.
translations.idiomatic: How much more this renewed Han of boundless extent, with you as its roof beam—this brooks no neglect.

sentences.zh: 且衍聞之，兵久則力屈，人悉則變生。

translations.literal: Moreover your servant Yan has heard: troops long deployed then strength is exhausted; people exhausted then change is born.
translations.idiomatic: Prolonged war exhausts an army; a spent people breeds revolt.

sentences.zh: 今邯鄲之賊未滅，真定之際複擾，而大將軍所部不過百里，守城不休，戰軍不息，兵革雲翔，百姓震駭，奈何自怠，不為深憂？

translations.literal: Now the bandits of Handan are not yet destroyed; the region of Zhending is again disturbed; yet the territory the grand marshal holds does not exceed a hundred li, garrisoning cities without rest, fighting armies without cease, weapons and armor cloud-soaring, the hundred surnames quake in alarm—how can you yourself slacken and not harbor deep worry?
translations.idiomatic: Handan still burns, Zhending stirs again, yet your district is barely a hundred li across, garrisons endless, battles unceasing, arms piled sky-high, the people terrified—how can you rest easy?

sentences.zh: 夫并州之地，東帶名關，北逼強胡，年穀獨孰，人庶多資，斯四戰之地，攻守之場也。

translations.literal: Now the land of Bing province, east girdled by famous passes, north pressed by mighty Hu; the year's grain alone ripens; the people and multitude have much to rely on—it is a place of battle on four sides, a field for attack and defense.
translations.idiomatic: Bingzhou is belted by great passes in the east, pressed by the Hu in the north, its harvests richest, its people resilient—a crossroads every army covets.

sentences.zh: 如其不虞，何以待之？

translations.literal: If there should be mischance, with what will you await it?
translations.idiomatic: If surprise strikes, what will you meet it with?

sentences.zh: 故曰“德不素積，人不為用。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said, 'Virtue not piled up in ordinary times, men will not be usable.'
translations.idiomatic: The proverb says: without stored virtue, men will not serve you.

sentences.zh: 備不豫具，難以應卒”。

translations.literal: If preparations are not laid in advance, it is hard to answer sudden crisis.'
translations.idiomatic: Without stockpiled readiness you cannot meet a sudden storm.'"

sentences.zh: 今生人之命，縣于將軍，將軍所杖，必須良才，宜改易非任，更選賢能。

translations.literal: Now the life of the living hangs from the general; what the general leans on must be good talent—it is fitting to change those wrongly placed and anew select the worthy and able.
translations.idiomatic: The people's lives hang on you; your staff must be worthy—replace the unfit and name better men.

sentences.zh: 夫十室之邑，必有忠信。

translations.literal: In a settlement of ten houses there is sure to be one loyal and trustworthy.
translations.idiomatic: Even a hamlet of ten holds loyal hearts.

sentences.zh: 審得其人，以承大將軍之明，雖則山澤之人，無不感德，思樂為用矣。

translations.literal: If truly you obtain such men to receive the grand marshal's enlightenment, though they be men of mountains and marshes, none will fail to feel virtue and wish joyfully to be employed.
translations.idiomatic: Win the right men under your banner and even hill folk will die for your kindness.

sentences.zh: 然後簡精銳之卒，發屯守之士，三軍既整，甲兵已具，相其土地之饒，觀其水泉之利，制屯田之術，習戰射之教，則威風遠暢，人安其業矣。

translations.literal: Then pick crack troops, call out garrison guards, once the three armies are ordered and armor and weapons provided, inspect where the land is rich, observe where springs and water profit, institute the method of frontier farming, drill them in battle and archery—then awesome might will reach far, and men will rest secure in their occupations.
translations.idiomatic: Then drill picked troops, open military farms on rich soil and good water, teach archery and shield—your awe will carry far and the people will farm in peace.

sentences.zh: 若鎮太原，撫上党，收百姓之歡心，樹名賢之良佐，天下無變，則足以顯聲譽，一朝有事，則可以建大功。

translations.literal: If you secure Taiyuan and soothe Shangdang, harvest the hundred surnames' glad hearts and plant worthy assistants of good name, when all under heaven has no change, then it suffices to display reputation; if in one morning there is affair, then you can establish great merit.
translations.idiomatic: Hold Taiyuan, win Shangdang, bind the people with good rule and wise aides—peace will burnish your name, crisis will let you win undying merit.

sentences.zh: 惟大將軍開日月之明，發深淵之慮，監《六經》之論，觀孫、吳之策，省群議之是非，詳眾士之白黑，以超《周南》之跡，垂《甘棠》之風，令夫功烈施於千載，富貴傳於無窮。

translations.literal: Only may the grand marshal open sun-and-moon brightness, issue deep-pool reflection, examine the discussions of the Six Classics, observe the stratagems of Sun and Wu, review whether the host of counsels is right or wrong, scrutinize black and white among the many gentlemen, so as to surpass the traces south of Zhou, let fall the wind of the sweet pear tree, cause husband merit and blaze to extend over a thousand ages, wealth and honor to transmit without end.
translations.idiomatic: Only open your mind like sun and moon, sound the Six Classics and Sun Wu's art, sift counsel true from false, men loyal from hollow, outshine the southern Zhou, leave a Duke of Shao's sweet-pear fame, and your glory will span the ages.

sentences.zh: 伊、望之策，何以加茲！

translations.literal: How can the plans of Yi Yin and Lü Wang add to this!
translations.idiomatic: What could Yi Yin or Lü Wang add beyond that?

sentences.zh: 永既素重衍，為且受使得自置偏裨，乃以衍為立漢將軍，領狼孟長，屯太原，與上党太守田邑等繕甲養士，扞衛並土。

translations.literal: Yong had long esteemed Yan; because he was about to receive mission and was allowed himself to appoint subordinate generals, he thereupon made Yan General Who Establishes Han, concurrently chief of Langmeng, encamped at Taiyuan, and together with grand administrator of Shangdang Tian Yi and others repaired armor and nurtured soldiers, defending Bing soil.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Yong, who already respected Feng Yan, let him name subordinates and made him General Who Establishes Han and magistrate of Langmeng, camped at Taiyuan with Tian Yi of Shangdang to arm the border.

sentences.zh: 衍聞之，委質為臣，無有二心；

translations.literal: Your servant Yan has heard: casting one's substance to be a minister, there is no second heart;
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that a subject who pledges loyalty has but one heart;

sentences.zh: 挈瓶之智，守不假器。

translations.literal: the wisdom that fills a pitcher guards and does not borrow vessels.
translations.idiomatic: the wit that fills a small jar does not pawn another's trust.

sentences.zh: 是以晏嬰臨盟，擬以曲戟，不易其辭；

translations.literal: Therefore Yan Ying at the covenant, threatened with a bent halberd, did not alter his words;
translations.idiomatic: Yan Ying at a league faced a halberd at his throat yet never bent his word;

sentences.zh: 謝息守郕，脅以晉、魯，不喪其邑。

translations.literal: Xie Xi guarding Cheng was coerced by Jin and Lu, yet did not lose his town.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Xi held Cheng against Jin and Lu and never lost his city.

sentences.zh: 由是言之，內無鉤頸之禍，外無桃萊之利，而被畔人之聲，蒙降城之恥，竊為左右羞之。

translations.literal: From this speaking, having within no misfortune of hooking the neck, without the profit of peach and lai, yet bearing the reputation of a rebel, wearing the shame of surrendering a city—your servant privately is ashamed for those at your left and right.
translations.idiomatic: Thus one may die without the halberd at one's neck yet gain no bribe of orchards—only the name of traitor and the shame of yielding a wall; I blush for your counselors.

sentences.zh: 且邾庶其竊邑畔君，以要大利，曰賤而必書；

translations.literal: Moreover Zhu Shuqi stole a town and rebelled against the lord to seek great profit—though base, the Spring and Autumn must record it;
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Shuqi seized a town to extort his prince—the Annals still records such baseness;

sentences.zh: 莒牟夷以土地求食，而名不滅。

translations.literal: Mou Yi of Ju sought food with land yet his name was not extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: Mou Yi of Ju traded land for safety yet kept his name.

sentences.zh: 是以大丈夫動則思禮，行則思義，未有背此而身名能全者也。

translations.literal: Therefore the great man in moving thinks of ritual, in acting thinks of righteousness; there has never been one who turned his back on these yet could preserve body and name whole.
translations.idiomatic: A gentleman weighs every step by rite and right; abandon those and neither life nor fame survives.

sentences.zh: 為伯玉深計，莫若與鮑尚書同情戮力，顯忠貞之節，立超世之功。

translations.literal: To plan deeply for Boyu, nothing is better than to share mind and join strength with Director Bao Yong, display the integrity of loyalty and steadfastness, and establish merit that transcends the age.
translations.idiomatic: For Tian Yi's good, join Bao Yong in one loyal effort and win a fame beyond your time.

sentences.zh: 如以尊親系累之故，能捐位投命，歸之尚書，大義既全，敵人紓怨，上不損剖符之責，下足救老幼之命，申眉高談，無愧天下。

translations.literal: If because of the burden of honored kin you can cast away office and hazard life, returning to the Director of the Secretariat, the great righteousness already whole, the enemy loosens resentment, above not damaging the responsibility of the split tally, below sufficing to save old and young lives, brows stretched in high discourse, without shame before all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: If kinship moves you to lay down command and return to Bao Yong, you keep the great right, slacken your foe's hate, honor your commission above, save your family below, and hold your head high before the world.

sentences.zh: 若乃貪上党之權，惜全邦之實，衍恐伯玉必懷周趙之憂，上黨複有前年之禍。

translations.literal: If on the other hand you are greedy for power in Shangdang and cling to the substance of a whole state, your servant Yan fears Boyu will harbor the worry of Zhou and Zhao, and Shangdang again have the disaster of the former year.
translations.idiomatic: Cling to Shangdang's power and the whole commandery's wealth, and I fear you court the fate of Zhou and Zhao and repeat last year's calamity.

sentences.zh: 昔晏平仲納延陵之誨，終免欒高之難；

translations.literal: In the past Yan Pingzhong accepted Yanling's instruction and in the end escaped the disaster of Luan and Gao;
translations.idiomatic: Yan Ying heeded Yanling's warning and escaped the Luan-Gao purge;

sentences.zh: 孫林父違穆子之戒，故陷終身之惡。

translations.literal: Sun Linfu disobeyed Muzi's warning, therefore fell into lifelong evil.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Linfu spurned Muzi's counsel and earned lifelong infamy.

sentences.zh: 以為伯玉聞此至言，必若刺心，自非嬰城而堅守，則策馬而不顧也。

translations.literal: Your servant thought that when Boyu heard this utmost speech, it would be like piercing the heart; unless he defended the city to the last, he would whip his horse and not look back.
translations.idiomatic: When Tian Yi hears this straight talk it should pierce his heart—either bolt the gates or gallop away without a backward glance.

sentences.zh: 聖人轉禍而為福，智士因敗以成勝，願自強于時，無與俗同。

translations.literal: Sages turn calamity into blessing; wise men by defeat achieve victory—I hope you will strengthen yourself to the times and not be the same as the vulgar.
translations.idiomatic: Sages turn ruin to gain; wise men ride defeat to victory—brace to the hour, sir, and do not drift with the mob.

sentences.zh: 邑報書曰：

translations.literal: Yi replied with a letter, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Tian Yi answered in writing:

sentences.zh: 僕雖駑怯，亦欲為人者也，豈苟貪生而畏死哉！

translations.literal: Though your servant is dull and timid, he also wishes to be human—how could he merely be greedy for life and fear death!
translations.idiomatic: I am slow and timid, yet I too am a man—do you think I cling to life from cowardice alone?

sentences.zh: 曲戟在頸，不易其心，誠僕志也。

translations.literal: With bent halberd at the neck, not to alter his heart—that is truly your servant's resolve.
translations.idiomatic: A halberd at my throat would not turn my heart—that is my vow.

sentences.zh: 間者，老母諸弟見執于軍，而邑安然不顧者，豈非重其節乎？

translations.literal: Recently, when old mother and younger brothers were seized by the army, yet Yi was calm and did not look to it—was it not because he weighed their integrity?
translations.idiomatic: When my mother and brothers were taken I held still—was that not because I weighed honor heavier?

sentences.zh: 若使人居天地，壽如金石，要長生而避死地可也。

translations.literal: If one dwelt between Heaven and Earth with life lasting as metal and stone, seeking long life and avoiding the place of death would be acceptable.
translations.idiomatic: Were our lives long as bronze, one might dodge death forever.

sentences.zh: 今百齡之期，未有能至，老壯之間，相去幾何。

translations.literal: Now the term of a hundred years, none can reach; between old and vigorous, how much distance is there?
translations.idiomatic: But none of us reaches a hundred; what span divides youth from age?

sentences.zh: 誠使故朝尚在，忠義可立，雖老親受戮，妻兒橫分，邑之願也。

translations.literal: If truly the former court still stood and loyalty could be established, though old kin suffered execution, wife and children were torn apart, that would be Yi's wish.
translations.idiomatic: If the old Han still lived and loyalty meant something, I would accept my kin's death and my family's ruin as my own choice.

sentences.zh: 間者，上黨黠賊，大眾圍城，義兵兩輩，入據井陘。

translations.literal: Recently the cunning bandits of Shangdang, a great host, besieged the city; righteous troops in two divisions entered and held Jingxing.
translations.idiomatic: Lately Shangdang's rebels ringed the city while loyal columns seized Jingxing.

sentences.zh: 邑親潰敵圍，拒擊宗正，自試智勇，非不能當。

translations.literal: Yi personally broke the enemy siege, resisted and struck the director of the imperial clan—testing wisdom and courage, it was not inability to match.
translations.idiomatic: I myself broke their lines and drove back the imperial clansman—rest assured I could fight.

sentences.zh: 誠知故朝為兵所害，新帝司徒已定三輔，隴西、北地從風回應。

translations.literal: Truly knowing the former court was harmed by arms, the new emperor's Minister of Education has already settled the Three Adjuncts, Longxi and Beidi turn in the wind and respond.
translations.idiomatic: But I saw the old regime crushed, the new emperor's minister secure the capital region, Longxi and Beidi rallying to him.

sentences.zh: 其事昭昭，日月經天，河海帶地，不足以比。

translations.literal: The affair is bright and bright—sun and moon thread the sky, rivers and seas gird the earth—not enough to compare.
translations.idiomatic: That fact shines like sun and moon; no river or sea is clearer.

sentences.zh: 死生有命，富貴在天。

translations.literal: Death and life have fate; wealth and honor lie with Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Life and death are fated; wealth rests with Heaven.

sentences.zh: 天下存亡，誠雲命也。

translations.literal: The survival or perishing of all under heaven is truly said to be fate.
translations.idiomatic: The empire's rise or fall is Heaven's sentence.

sentences.zh: 邑雖沒身，能如命何？

translations.literal: Though Yi lose his body, what can he do against fate?
translations.idiomatic: Though I die for it, what quarrel have I with fate?

sentences.zh: 夫人道之本，有恩有義，義有所宜，恩有所施。

translations.literal: Now the root of the human way has favor and has righteousness; righteousness has what is fitting, favor has what is to be bestowed.
translations.idiomatic: Human relations rest on kindness and right; each has its proper measure.

sentences.zh: 君臣大義，母子至恩。

translations.literal: The great righteousness of ruler and minister, the utmost favor of mother and son.
translations.idiomatic: Between lord and subject stands great duty; between mother and child stands deepest love.

sentences.zh: 今故主已亡，義其誰為；

translations.literal: Now the former lord is already gone; for whom is the righteousness?
translations.idiomatic: My old master is dead—who is left to serve in his name?

sentences.zh: 老母拘執，恩所當留。

translations.literal: Old mother is seized and held; by favor it is fitting to remain.
translations.idiomatic: My mother is hostage—duty binds me to stay.

sentences.zh: 而厲以貪權，誘以策馬，抑其利心，必其不顧，何其愚乎！

translations.literal: Yet to press with greed for power, entice with mounting a horse, suppress their profit-seeking heart, and require their not looking back—how is that foolish!
translations.idiomatic: Yet you bait me with rank and spur me with flight, as if I were blind to shame—how foolish you must think me!

sentences.zh: 邑年三十，曆位卿士，性少嗜欲，情厭事為。

translations.literal: Yi at thirty years passed through stations to minister rank; by nature he has few desires, his disposition tires of affairs.
translations.idiomatic: I am thirty, risen to high office, sick of intrigue, scant of appetite for power.

sentences.zh: 況今位尊身危，財多命殆，鄙人知之，何疑君子？

translations.literal: How much more now position is exalted and body in peril, wealth much and life at risk—vulgar men know this, why doubt a gentleman?
translations.idiomatic: High rank now means peril, great wealth means a short rope—even fools see it; do you doubt a gentleman does?

sentences.zh: 君長、敬通揭節垂組，自相署立。

translations.literal: Lord Junzhang and Jingtong let fall their credentials and hung their seals, appointing themselves.
translations.idiomatic: You, Bao Yong, and Feng Yan hung up seals and named yourselves generals.

sentences.zh: 蓋仲由使門人為臣，孔子譏其欺天。

translations.literal: This is like Zhong You making his disciples play minister—Confucius ridiculed him for deceiving Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: That is Zhong You styling his pupils as ministers—Confucius called it cheating Heaven.

sentences.zh: 君長據位兩州，加以一郡，而河東畔國，兵不入彘，上黨見圍，不窺大谷，宗正臨境，莫之能援。

translations.literal: Lord Junzhang holds two provinces plus a commandery, yet Hedong rebelled against the state and troops did not enter Zhi; Shangdang was besieged yet did not peer into Dagu; the director of the imperial clan pressed the border yet none could rescue.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Yong, you held two provinces and a commandery, yet when Hedong rebelled your men never reached Zhi, when Shangdang was ringed you never looked to Dagu Pass, and when the clansman's army came you sent no aid.

sentences.zh: 兵威屈辱，國權日損，三王背畔，赤眉害主，未見兼行倍道之赴，若墨翟累繭救宋，申包胥重胝存楚，衛女馳歸唁兄之志。

translations.literal: Military might was humiliated, state authority daily diminished; the three kings turned rebel, the Red Eyebrows harmed the lord—yet your servant has not seen a doubled-road march to the rescue like Mo Di's callused heels saving Song, Shen Baoxu's blistered feet preserving Chu, or the Wei lady galloping home to mourn her brother's resolve.
translations.idiomatic: Arms were shamed, the throne's power ebbed, princes turned traitor, the sovereign fell to the Red Eyebrows—yet I saw no Mo Di racing to save Song, no Shen Baoxu weeping Chu home, no sister of Wei galloping to her brother's grave.

sentences.zh: 主亡一歲，莫知定所，虛冀妄言，苟肆鄙塞。

translations.literal: The lord has been gone a year; none knows where he is settled; vain hope and reckless words merely vent vulgar blockage.
translations.idiomatic: Our master is a year dead with no tomb we know; empty boasts only clog honest ears.

sentences.zh: 未能事生，安能事死？

translations.literal: Unable to serve the living, how can one serve the dead?
translations.idiomatic: If we could not save him alive, how dare we posture for him dead?

sentences.zh: 未知為臣，焉知為主？

translations.literal: Not having known how to be a minister, how can one know how to be a lord?
translations.idiomatic: If you never learned to serve, how can you claim to rule?

sentences.zh: 豈厭為臣子，思為君父乎！

translations.literal: Could it be that you are weary of being minister and son and wish to be lord and father!
translations.idiomatic: Surely you do not weary of being a subject and crave the father's throne!

sentences.zh: 欲搖太山而蕩北海，事敗身危，要思邑言。

translations.literal: You wish to shake Mount Tai and rock the North Sea; when the affair fails and body is in peril, you should think on Yi's words.
translations.idiomatic: You would move Taishan and stir the northern sea—when the plot breaks and your life hangs by a thread, remember what Tian Yi told you.

sentences.zh: 衍不從。

translations.literal: Yan did not follow.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan would not heed him.

sentences.zh: 或訛言更始隨赤眉在北，永、衍信之，故屯兵界休，方移書上党，雲皇帝在雍，以惑百姓。

translations.literal: Some spread rumor that Gengshi was with the Red Eyebrows in the north; Yong and Yan believed it, therefore encamped troops at Jiexiu, and were about to send a letter up to Shangdang saying the emperor was at Yong, in order to delude the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: Rumors placed the Gengshi Emperor with the Red Eyebrows in the north; Bao Yong and Feng Yan believed them, camped at Jiexiu, and drafted letters to Shangdang claiming the court was at Yong to confuse the people.

sentences.zh: 永遣弟升及子媚張舒誘降涅城，舒家在上黨，邑悉系之。

translations.literal: Yong sent his younger brother Sheng and son Mei, Zhang Shu, to entice and surrender Nie city; Shu's family was in Shangdang, and Yi seized them all.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Yong sent his brother Sheng and son Bao Mei with Zhang Shu to win Nie by defection; Zhang Shu's kin lived in Shangdang, and Tian Yi arrested every one of them.

sentences.zh: 又書勸永降，永不答，自是與邑有隙。

translations.literal: Again he wrote urging Yong to surrender; Yong did not reply; from this he had a rift with Yi.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote again urging Bao Yong to yield; Yong did not answer—so the two were enemies thereafter.

sentences.zh: 邑字伯玉，馮翊人也，後為漁陽太守。

translations.literal: Yi, courtesy name Boyu, was a man of Fengyi; later he became grand administrator of Yuyang.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Yi, styled Boyu, was from Fengyi and later served as grand administrator of Yuyang.

sentences.zh: 永、衍審知更始已歿，乃共罷兵，幅巾降於河內。

translations.literal: Yong and Yan ascertained that Gengshi had already died; thereupon together they disbanded the army, with headcloth bound surrendered in Henei.
translations.idiomatic: When Bao Yong and Feng Yan learned Gengshi was dead, they disbanded their host and surrendered to Han at Henei in plain cloth.

sentences.zh: 帝怨衍等不時至，永以立功得贖罪，遂任用之，而衍獨見黜。

translations.literal: The emperor resented Yan and the others for not arriving in timely fashion; Yong by establishing merit obtained ransom from crime, thereupon was employed and used, while Yan alone was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu resented their late arrival; Bao Yong bought pardon with merit and kept office, but Feng Yan alone was cast aside.

sentences.zh: 永謂衍曰：“昔高祖賞季布之罪，誅丁固之功。

translations.literal: Yong said to Yan, "In the past Gaozu rewarded Ji Bu's crime and executed Ding Gu's merit.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Yong said, "Gaozu pardoned Ji Bu and executed Ding Gu for ingratitude.

sentences.zh: 今遭明主，亦何憂哉！

translations.literal: Now you meet an enlightened ruler—what is there to worry about!"
translations.idiomatic: You now serve an enlightened sovereign—what have you to fear!"

sentences.zh: ”衍曰：“記有之，人有挑其鄰人之妻者，挑其長者，長者詈之，挑其少者，少者報之，後其夫死而取其長者。

translations.literal: " Yan said, "The records have it: there was a man who teased his neighbor's wives; teasing the elder, the elder cursed him; teasing the younger, the younger responded to him; afterward when her husband died he took the elder.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan answered, "The books tell of a man who flirted with a neighbor's two wives: the elder scolded him, the younger welcomed him, and when their husband died he married the one who had cursed him.

sentences.zh: 或謂之曰：‘夫非罵爾者邪？

translations.literal: Someone said to him, 'Was not your husband the one who cursed you?'
translations.idiomatic: People asked, 'Was that not the woman who reviled you?'

sentences.zh: ’曰：‘在人欲其報我，在我欲其罵人也。

translations.literal: ' He said, 'In others I desire that they respond to me; in myself I desire that they curse people.'
translations.idiomatic: She said, 'When others court me I want devotion; when I court others I want honesty—even if it stings.'

sentences.zh: ’夫天命難知，人道易守，守道之臣，何患死亡？

translations.literal: ' Now Heaven's mandate is hard to know, the human way easy to keep—a minister who keeps the Way, why worry about death?
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's will is dark, but a man's course is clear—why should a minister who keeps the Way fear death?"

sentences.zh: ”頃之，帝以衍為曲陽令，誅斬劇賊郭勝等，降五千餘人，論功當封，以讒毀，故賞不行。

translations.literal: " Before long the emperor appointed Yan magistrate of Quyang; he executed and beheaded the fierce bandit Guo Sheng and others, surrendering more than five thousand; by merit in discussion he ought to have been enfeoffed, but because of slander and defamation, therefore reward was not carried out.
translations.idiomatic: Soon the emperor named him magistrate of Quyang; he executed bandits like Guo Sheng and took five thousand surrenders—merit enough for a fief, but slander blocked any reward.

sentences.zh: 建武六年日食，衍上書陳八事：其一曰顯文德，二曰褒武烈，三曰修舊功，四曰招俊傑，五曰明好惡，六曰簡法令，七曰差秩祿，八曰撫邊境。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Jianwu there was a solar eclipse; Yan submitted a memorial stating eight matters: the first called for displaying civil virtue, the second for praising martial glory, the third for repairing old merit, the fourth for summoning outstanding men, the fifth for clarifying likes and dislikes, the sixth for simplifying laws and orders, the seventh for grading rank salary, the eighth for soothing the border regions.
translations.idiomatic: After a solar eclipse in Jianwu 6 he memorialized eight points: promote culture, honor arms, restore old services, recruit talent, clarify rewards and punishments, streamline law, grade salaries, and pacify the frontier.

sentences.zh: 書奏，帝將召見。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, the emperor was about to summon him for audience.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read it and meant to receive him at court.

sentences.zh: 初，衍為狼孟長，以罪摧陷大姓令狐略。

translations.literal: At first, when Yan was chief of Langmeng, by a crime he had crushed and trapped the great surname Linghu Lue.
translations.idiomatic: Years before, as magistrate of Langmeng, he had framed the magnate Linghu Lue.

sentences.zh: 是時，略為司空長史，讒之于尚書令王護、尚書周生豐曰：“衍所以求見者，欲毀君也。

translations.literal: At that time Lue was senior clerk to the Minister of Works; he slandered him to Director of the Secretariat Wang Hu and Director Zhou Sheng Feng, saying, "The reason Yan seeks audience is that he wishes to destroy you gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Linghu Lue was now clerk to the minister of works; he told Wang Hu and Zhou Sheng Feng that Feng Yan meant to ruin them at audience.

sentences.zh: ”護等懼之，即共排間，衍遂不得入。

translations.literal: " Hu and the others feared him; immediately together they blocked and estranged him, and Yan thereupon could not gain entry.
translations.idiomatic: They blocked his path to the throne out of fear.

sentences.zh: 後衛尉陰興、新陽侯陰就以外戚貴顯，深敬重衍，衍遂與之交結，是由為諸王所聘請，尋為司隸從事。

translations.literal: Later Commandant of the Guards Yin Xing and Marquis of Xinyang Yin Jiu, by maternal kinship noble and eminent, deeply respected Yan; Yan thereupon associated and bound himself with them, whereby he was recruited by the various princes; soon he became a clerk to the Metropolitan Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Later Yin Xing and Yin Jiu, powerful in-laws, befriended him; he entered the princes' service and became a clerk under the metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 帝懲西京外戚賓客，故皆以法繩之，大者抵死徙，其餘至貶黜。

translations.literal: The emperor, warning against Western Capital maternal kin and clients, therefore all applied the law to them; the great went to death or exile, the rest to demotion and dismissal.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu tightened the law on in-laws and their clients as Western Han had not: the worst died or were exiled, the rest stripped of rank.

sentences.zh: 衍由此得罪，嘗自詣獄，有詔赦不問。

translations.literal: Yan by this incurred guilt; he once went in person to prison; there was an edict pardoning and not prosecuting.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan fell under the edict; he turned himself in to jail but received a general pardon.

sentences.zh: 西歸故郡，閉門自保，不也複與親故通。

translations.literal: He returned west to his old commandery, closed the gate and preserved himself, and no longer communicated with kin and old friends.
translations.idiomatic: He went home to his native commandery, shut his gate, and broke with kin and old companions.
